paragraphs.context: semr SEMI E174-0819 SPECIFICATION FOR WAFER JOB MANAGEMENT (WJM) This Standard was technically approved by the Information & Control Global Technical Committee. This edition was approved for publication by the global Audits and Reviews Subcommittee on May 7, 2019 and August 19. 2019. Available at www.scmivicws.org and ww,v.scmi.org in August 2019: originally published November 2016: previously published June 2018. 1 Purpose 1.1 The purpose of this Standard is to provide a communication method which observes and/or controls every single wafer (including its related infonnation) on equipment individually (and independently rather than a member of a lot) through single management point from the host. 1.2 The purpose of this Standard is to introduce Wafer Job (WJ) in order to observe and/or control a wafer (and its infonnation including context information from related actions such as pre-processing, processing, and post processing steps) in the equipment independently. 1.3 The purpose of this Standard is to provide Wafer Job Object (WJOBJ) model so that the information that should be managed in conjunction with the W J can be encapsulated in the object and can be accessed from the host. 1.4 The purpose oftbis Standard is to introduce Wafer Job Management Job (WJMJ) in order to manage multiple W Js in the equipment. 1.5 The purpose of this Standard is to provide Wafer Job Management Job Object (WJMJOBJ) model so that the information that should be managed in conjunction with the WJMJ can be encapsulated in the object and can be accessed from the host.

paragraphs.context: Objecrs 6.1.1 Whenever the equipment is required to know about specific kinds of entities and required to manage information concerning these entities. it is useful to treat these entities as objects that comply with the basic requirements of SEMI E39, Object Services Standard (OSS). This is especially true whenever there are a large number of objects of a given type or when the entities are transient rather than permanent. Ln both cases, it is difficult to describe a general way for the host and equipment to specify which particular entity is referenced and to get information related only to a specific one out of many. 6.1.2 By defining these entities as objects that comply with OSS. it is only necessary for the host to specify the type of object and its specific identifier in order to inquire about one or more properties of the specific entity of interest. 6.1.3 Object Properties 6.1.3.1 A property (attribute) is information about an individual object that is presented as a name and value pair. The name is a formally reserved text string that represents the property, and the value is the current setting for that property. 6.1.3.2 Properties shall be accessible to the host via the service GetAttr. Using SEMI E39 Object Services Standard, for example, it is possible to: get the list of IDs for the current objects at the equipment. and get the specified properties for one or more individual objects. 6.1.4 Rules for Object Properties Attributes wirb RO (Read Only) access

paragraphs.context: 6.2 State Model Methodology 6.2.1 A state model consists of four elements: a State Model Diagram, a State Model Definition Table, a State Definition. and a State Transition Table. 6.2.2 State Model Diagram - The diagram of the state model uses the Harel State Chart notation. An overview of this notation is presented in an Appendix of SEMI E30. The definition of this notation is presented in Science of Computer Programming 8, "Statecharts: A Visual Formalism for Complex Systems." by D. Harel, 1987.1 6.2.3 State Model Definition Table - The State Model Definition Table used in this Standard bas the following format. The value of the NoPJinterrupt attribution provides the indication of whether or not this mode is enabled, and is initially set to 'On'. In the case this mode is not supported then the oPJinterrupt attribute is not supported. The behavior of the equipment with respect to prohibiting insertion when this mode is not supported is beyond the scope of this Standard. SEMI E174-0819 SEMI 2016, 2019 l WJM C; eated CJ I

paragraphs.context: Abatement Systems .................................................. 19 Related Infom1ation 3 Energy Saving Mode EtherCA T Communication for Pump Systems ..................................... 21 Related lnfonnation 4 Energy Savrng Mode EtherCA T Commurucation for Abatement Systems ............................. 24 SEMI E175-1116 SEMI 2016 semr 1 Purpose I.I The purpose of this Specification is to define communication between production equipment and the subsystem to enable energy saving mode (reduced energy consumption rate) operation by using sleep level transitions. A means to manage and communicate the subsystem status is provided for the production equipment to initiate (and terminate) subsystem energy saving mode operation in a coordinated manner. 2 Scope 2.1 This Specification defines communication and behavior between the production equipment and each subsystem to facilitate energy saving mode operation or sleep level management. 2.2 This Specification addresses: Definition of subsystem energy saving mode operation in the form of a state model. Communication to manage the subsystem's energy saving mode operation via sleep levels. NOTI E: SEMI Standards and Safety Guidelines do not purport to address aJI safety issues associated with their use. It is the responsibility of the users of the Documents to establish appropriate safety and health practices. and determine the applicability of regulatory or other limitations prior to use. 3 Limitations 3.1 Safety interlocks are not part of this Specification. 3.2 This Specification applies to subsystems capable of transition to one or more energy saving modes or sleep levels on command for the purpose of energy reduction operation. 3.3 This Specification applies to only one-on-one connections between

paragraphs.context: to use. 3 Limitations 3.1 Safety interlocks are not part of this Specification. 3.2 This Specification applies to subsystems capable of transition to one or more energy saving modes or sleep levels on command for the purpose of energy reduction operation. 3.3 This Specification applies to only one-on-one connections between the production equipment and a subsystem. Multiple equipment connections to one subsystem are outside the scope of this Document. 3.4 This Specification does not define the amount of energy saving to be attained or how that energy saving is to be achieved. 3.5 The energy reduction rates based on sleep levels or the reporting of the supplier's subsystem defined energy savings are not specified in this Document. 3.6 The specific protocol for messaging between production equipment and the subsystem including the detailed message structure is not part in this Specification. NOTE 1: Related Information sections arc included in this Document to show examples of potential dry contact and EthcrCA T protocol communication solutions. These proposed solutions need to be validated via implementation. 3. 7 Additional state machines and internal controls between production equipment and the subsystems may exist and are beyond the scope of this Specification. 4 Referenced Standards and Documents 4.1 SEMI Standards and Safef)' Guidelines SEMI Et67 - Specification for Equipment Energy Saving Mode Communication (EESM) SEMI S23 - Guide for Conservation of Energy, Utilities and Materials Used by Semiconductor Manufacturing Equipment NOTICE: Unless otherwise indicated, all documents cited shall be the latest published versions. 5 Terminology 5.1

A subsystem may be a constituent of another subsystem. 5.2.6 subsystem idle mode - the mode of operation where the subsystem is ready to transition into process mode without significant delay, this transition may be made with or without requiring any specific control command. NOTE 3: It is possible that a subsystem has no idle mode. 5.2.7 subsystem process mode - the mode of operation where the subsystem is energized, and performing its intended function (such as abating gas, pumping gas, or controlling temperature). 5.2.8 subsystem sleep mode - the mode, entered by the subsystem in response to an external command, in which there will be a delay to return to ready but it is otherwise ready for processing. paragraphs.context: can be more easily understood. 7.2 Basic Concepts 7.2.1 The subsystem energy saving mode capability provides the opportunity for the production equipment to manage the reduction of energy use by a subsystem and achieve different rates of energy consumption depending on the length of time the production equipment does not require the subsystem for production or a related task. 7.2.2 The amount of energy consumed by a subsystem varies over time depending on the process load and mode of operation. A subsystem is likely to conswne the most energy when the production equipment is processing material. However, a significant amount of energy is still being consumed when the production equipment is not being used for processing material. This Specification addresses the opportunities for subsystem reduced energy operation, in particular when the production equipment is not currently in use and the factory has no plans to use the production equipment resources for an extended amount of time. 7.2.3 When SEMI E1 67 is used, the host will indicate to the production equipment how long it will not be needed for production. This allows the production equipment to reduce the rate of energy usage during this time. This Specification provides an opportunity for energy savings that the production equipment may gain by standardizing the request for reduced energy operation to its subsystems. This Specification does not specify how these savings are achieved but provides a method to coordinate such savings. 7.2.4 It is assumed that idle subsystems can consume less energy than

It is expected that further reduction of energy consumption rates could come at the price of a time delay to recover from the lower energy use. 7.3 Energy Saving Mode Operation 7.3.1 This Specification standardizes communications related to subsystem's reduced energy mode operation. lt does not attempt to constrain the amount of energy savings or the methods used to achieve a reduction in the rate of energy consumption. This Specification allows the production equipment and suppliers of subsystems as much leeway as possible to optimize the energy reduction implementation. SEMI E175-1116 SEMI 2016 semr 1.4 Subsystem Sleep Levels 7.4.1 A subsystem may provide more than one sleep level which could result in additional energy savings. While this Specification does not require the support of sleep levels, it does provide a mechanism for commanding the subsystem to a sleep level if the subsystem supports that concept. 7.4.2 Where multiple levels of sleep are supported by the subsystem, the production equipment requests the level of sleep to be used in each case. paragraphs.context: the production equipment is idJe and determines based on a host request that no material will be processed for a given amount of time. The production equipment decides that one or more subsystems can provide energy consumption savings. The production equipment requests these subsystems to operate at reduced energy consumption rate (sleep level) based on the time the subsystem will not be required for production. Each subsystem accepts or rejects the production equipment sleep level request. Subsystem rate of energy consumption is reduced. Each subsystem that accepted the sleep request remains in sleep mode until commanded to wake up (assuming no fault occurs). Allowing enough lead time for the subsystem to wake up, the production equipment sends a wake up request to each subsystem that was commanded to sleep. Each subsystem begins the waking up process. When the subsystem reaches the SubsystemReady state as specified in 9.3, it acknowledges to the production equipment that it is ready for process. 8 Prerequisites 8.1 The Subsystem Energy Saving Mode capability requires a communication protocol or signals to support exchange of requests or messages between production equipment and subsystem to manage the energy reduction. paragraphs.context: 9.4.3 WakeUp Request 9.4.3.1 The WakeUp request commands the subsystem to begin the process of transitioning from sleep mode back to SubsystemReady (see Transition 3 in Table 7). There are no current requirements of input parameters for the WakeUp request. 9.4.3.2 The subsystem notification in response to the WakeUp request indicates that the request has been accepted or rejected. Acceptance means that a reduced rate of energy use has concluded and that the subsystem is returning to normal operation. If rejected, the subsystem's responds with an event or by setting a warning. Some subsystems may choose to acknowledge acceptance via a change of one or more of its status signals (see Table 8). 9.4.3.3 If the WakeUp request is accepted by the subsystem, and successfully concludes sleep mode, the subsystem notifies the production equipment that it has reached its SubsystemReady state or one of its substates by issuing an event or setting a signal that indicates that the subsystem is ready to process (see Table 8). paragraphs.context: Low Speed (Sleep Mode) control sub-index. Using the index 0x7nn0 for the pump being commanded, the Boolean value of the sub-index 0x04 is changed from O to I commanding the pump to run in Low Speed (Sleep Mode) to save energy. When this value is toggled back from I to 0; the pump stops operating at reduced rate of energy and returns to onnal operation (Process Mode) as indicated in Table R3-I . SEMI E175-1 116 SEMI 2016 semr RJ-3.2.2 Energy Saving Mode using Sleep Levels RJ-3.2.2.1 When the pump provides multiple sleep levels; the multiple sleep level setting (unsigned integer) value is used to command the pump to operate at a lower energy level. The index 0x7nn0 of the pump being commanded to go into a sleep level is used by setting the sub-index Ox 11 value as indicated in Table RJ-2. The default setting for this value is equivalent to the pump's default Sleep Mode Level 0. When the pump provides multiple sleep levels the Multiple Sleep Mode Level Setting value is selected between 0 and any of the sleep levels implemented by the pump. These two sub-index values remain in that setting until the subsystem is commanded to WakeUp and returns to nonnal operation (Process Mode). R 1-4.3 There are two types of RPC calls in gRPC. All calls are initiated by the gRPC client. Unary-The client sends a request and blocks until the server sends a response. This method call is synchronous and does not support stream messages. Streaming - The software receiving the data in the stream does not need to wait until all the messages are delivered. For .proto files related to SEMI Standards, the following style guide will be used: Use PascalCase (with an initial capital) for: o message names (e.g., BasicParameterValue) o enumerated type names (e.g., JobTypeEnum) o service names and RPC method names (e.g., GetPlanDefinition) For field names, use lower snake case (each word is in lower case with an underscore between words) (e.g., sint32_item). Use underscores with upper case for enumeration value names (e.g .. GREATER_THA _EQUAL_TO or OPERA TOR_ TYPE_ UNSPECIFIED). Use pluralized names for repeated fields (e.g., array_ values or enumerated_strings). Any attempts to send or receive a message on a stream parameter object that is marked done will result in an error. All the fields in a oneof structure share the same memory, and at most one field can be set at the same time. Setting any member of the oneof fields clears all the other members automatically. You can check which value in a oneof field is specified (if any) using a special case() or WhichOneof() method, depending on the target development language. R3-7 Extending Functionality RJ-7.1 With XML, SEMI El38 stated that certain items could be refined and extended. This could be done because the XML are string notations. Protocol Buffers cannot be as easily extended. As a result, any extensions to error data structure, type definitions, and primitive data types will need to be integrated in the Protocol Buffer Common Components Standard, or defined as separate Protocol Buffers objects in the appropriate standard.
